evidence_text: The study analyzes 33,596 agentic pull requests from five coding agents and 6,618 human pull requests.
source_excerpt: "We use the AIDev-pop subset of the AIDev dataset ... which contains 33,596 curated agentic PRs (APRs) from five major autonomous coding agents: OpenAI Codex (21,799 PRs), Devin (4,827 PRs), GitHub Copilot (4,970 PRs), Cursor (1,541 PRs), and Claude Code (459 PRs). We also use a dataset of 6,618 human PRs (HPRs)"

evidence_text: The APR data spans 2024-12 to 2025-07, while the HPR data spans 2025-01 to 2025-06.
source_excerpt: "The APRs span 2024-12 to 2025-07 and the HPRs 2025-01 to 2025-06, covering comparable periods."

evidence_text: Agentic PRs had a lower merge rate than human PRs, at 76.80% versus 82.82%.
source_excerpt: "APRs have a lower merge rate than HPRs (76.80% vs. 82.82%, p < 0.001)"

evidence_text: Agentic PRs had smaller median change footprints than human PRs in commits, files changed, and directories changed, but not in lines changed.
source_excerpt: "APRs have ... fewer commits (median 1 vs. 2, p < 0.001), files (median 3 vs. 4, p < 0.001), and directories (median 1 vs. 2, p < 0.001). The number of changed lines does not differ significantly (p = 0.131)."

evidence_text: In logistic regression, each additional commit was associated with about a 5% reduction in merge odds.
source_excerpt: "Logistic regression indicates that the number of commits is a strong negative predictor of merging (OR = 0.95, p < 0.001), with each additional commit reducing merge odds by about 5%"

evidence_text: Agentic PRs had documentation and test tasks as their third and fourth most common categories, while human PRs had chore and build tasks in those positions.
citation: Pham and Ghaleb 2026, §2.3 RQ1.1 and Table 2
source_excerpt: "Table 2 shows that feature (feat) and bug fix (fix) tasks are the two most common task types for both groups, but the third and fourth most frequent tasks differ: APRs focus more on documentation and test tasks, whereas HPRs handle more build and chore tasks."
note: The source presents this task-type distribution as part of the explanation for why APRs have fewer commits per PR, but it does not establish that explanation as a demonstrated causal mechanism.
caveat: The source presents this task-type distribution as part of the explanation for why APRs have fewer commits per PR, but it does not establish that explanation as a demonstrated causal mechanism.

evidence_text: Files added by agentic PRs were modified later at a higher rate and much sooner than files added by human PRs.
citation: Pham and Ghaleb 2026, §2.3 RQ1.2 and Table 3
source_excerpt: "APRs show a higher file-modification ratio than HPRs (14.36% vs. 3.36%, p < 0.001) and are modified far earlier (median 0.88 days vs. 23 days, p < 0.001)."

evidence_text: Symbols introduced by agentic PRs had a higher churn rate and were removed much sooner than symbols introduced by human PRs.
citation: Pham and Ghaleb 2026, §2.3 RQ1.2 and Table 3
source_excerpt: "Among 7,338 APR symbols and 8,089 HPR symbols, APRs exhibit a higher churn rate (7.33% vs. 4.10%, p < 0.001) and their symbols are removed much sooner (median 3 days vs. 34 days, p < 0.001)."

evidence_text: The description-alignment analysis filtered PR text to English and evaluated 647 agentic PRs and 571 human PRs.
source_excerpt: "We filter PR text for English, as it constitutes the vast majority of the dataset (≈98%), and to ensure linguistic consistency ... yielding 22,633 APRs and 4,009 HPRs. From these, we sample 647 APRs and 571 HPRs"

evidence_text: Agentic PRs outperformed human PRs on commit-level message alignment by both patch-message semantic similarity and LLM-based consistency score.
source_excerpt: "APRs achieve higher commit-level alignment than HPRs both in semantic similarity (median 0.72 vs. 0.68, p < 0.001) and in LLM-as-a-judge scores (median 4/10 vs. 2/10), with p < 0.001 and effect sizes of ε2 ≈ 0.12 and 0.17, respectively."

evidence_text: Agentic PRs scored lower than human PRs on PR-level PR-commit similarity, with medians of 0.86 versus 0.88.
source_excerpt: "In contrast, at the PR level, APRs score lower than humans in PR–commit similarity (0.86 vs. 0.88, p < 0.001), but with an effect size of ε2 ≈ 0.04."
note: This evidence introduces a tension with the stronger agent performance at commit level: the source reports better local message alignment for agents but weaker PR-level summarization, and the PR-level effect size is small.
caveat: This evidence introduces a tension with the stronger agent performance at commit level: the source reports better local message alignment for agents but weaker PR-level summarization, and the PR-level effect size is small.

evidence_text: Commit message length was the strongest predictor of a good agentic PR description, with maximum SHAP value 0.57.
source_excerpt: "Commit message length is the strongest predictor of a good APR description (max SHAP: 0.57), followed by PR title and PR body length (max SHAP: 0.21 and 0.20, respectively)."

evidence_text: Higher-quality agentic PR descriptions were associated with commit messages of at least 13 words, PR titles of at least 7 words, and PR bodies of at least 80 words.
source_excerpt: "As shown in Figure 2, high-quality APR descriptions are strongly associated with commit messages ≥ 13 words, PR titles ≥ 7 words, and PR bodies ≥ 80 words."

evidence_text: The study states that its quality measures are proxy metrics that may miss factual correctness, and its classifier recalled only 31.9% of good descriptions.
citation: Pham and Ghaleb 2026, §4 Threats to validity
source_excerpt: "We use the AIDev-pop subset of the AIDev dataset ... which contains 33,596 curated agentic PRs (APRs) from five major autonomous coding agents: OpenAI Codex (21,799 PRs), Devin (4,827 PRs), GitHub Copilot (4,970 PRs), Cursor (1,541 PRs), and Claude Code (459 PRs). We also use a dataset of 6,618 human PRs (HPRs)"
note:
This caveat limits how strongly the reported alignment and predictor results can be treated as direct measures of true PR-description quality.
